serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of honeydew sago is approximately 200g, typically served chilled as a dessert or snack in Singapore hawker centres and dessert stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Honeydew Sago — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Honeydew Sago (F02050009). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this exact record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — sago and tapioca starch analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for honeydew sago as a composite dish. GI of 58 is estimated based on sago pearls (GI ~70 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the low total carbohydrate content (4.7g/100g) and the presence of coconut milk fat which slows gastric emptying. The input record cited GI 58 (medium) with GL 2.7 from an embedded GI database; this is consistent with the analogous sago-based dessert estimate. GL per serving recalculated as: (58 × 4.7g carbohydrate per 100g × 200g serving) / 100 / 100 = 5.45, rounded to 5.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4mg/100g) and low sugar (4.3g/100g); saturated fat from coconut milk warrants portion awareness
hypertension.guidance: Honeydew sago is suitable for individuals with hypertension given its negligible sodium content (4mg/100g); however, portion size should be limited to 150–200g to manage saturated fat intake from coconut milk, and versions with added condensed milk or sugar syrup should be avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (58) from sago pearls offset by low total carbohydrate load (4.7g/100g); refined starch with minimal fibre requires portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Honeydew sago has a medium GI of 58 but a low glycemic load per standard serving due to its low carbohydrate density (4.7g/100g); individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portions to 150g and avoid preparations with added condensed milk or sugar syrup to prevent glycaemic excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~2.4g/100g estimated) is the primary cardiovascular concern; low sodium and low calorie density are favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from coconut milk (estimated ~2.4g/100g) is the primary cardiovascular concern in honeydew sago; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150g and choose preparations using light coconut milk to reduce saturated fat intake, while benefiting from the dish's low sodium and low calorie profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Coconut milk saturated fat is pro-inflammatory; absence of meaningful omega-3, polyphenols, or fermented components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Honeydew sago is not recommended as a regular choice for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory saturated fat from coconut milk and the absence of meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients; occasional small portions (100g) may be acceptable if overall dietary saturated fat is well controlled.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honeydew sago (蜜瓜西米露) is a popular Singaporean chilled dessert consisting of diced honeydew melon, cooked sago pearls, and coconut milk, typically served cold. The dish is a staple at hawker centres and dessert stalls across Singapore and is enjoyed as a light, refreshing sweet course. Sago pearls are small translucent spheres made from tapioca or palm starch that become soft and gelatinous when boiled; they provide a mild, slightly chewy texture that contrasts with the crisp, juicy honeydew melon. Coconut milk adds a rich, creamy sweetness and a subtle tropical flavour. The dessert is low in calories (46 kcal/100g), low in sodium (4mg/100g), and moderate in fat (2.8g/100g), with the fat primarily derived from coconut milk saturated fat (~2.4g/100g estimated). Total carbohydrates are low at 4.7g/100g with sugar at 4.3g/100g, making it relatively light compared to many other desserts. The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium) based on sago starch, but the glycemic load per standard 200g serving is moderate at approximately 5.4. Clinically, this dessert is suitable for most individuals in moderate portions; those with type 2 diabetes should monitor portion size due to the medium GI of sago, and those with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis should be mindful of saturated fat from coconut milk. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal. It is commonly served as a dessert or afternoon snack and is particularly popular during warm weather.

Is honeydew sago suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F02050009) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match to the canonical Singapore HPB record for honeydew sago. GI is an analogous estimate based on sago starch from the University of Sydney GI Database, consistent with the input record's embedded GI value of 58.
notes: saturated_fat_g of 2.4g/100g is estimated based on coconut milk being the primary fat source in this dish; the HPB record reports total fat as 2.8g/100g but does not separately itemise saturated fat — this estimate is consistent with the health_suitability notes in the input record. cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0 as this is a plant-based dessert with no animal-derived ingredients contributing cholesterol. The dietary tag 'keto' present in the input record has been removed as this dish contains sago pearls (refined starch) and is not consistent with ketogenic dietary criteria. GL per serving recalculated as (GI 58 × 4.7g carbohydrate/100g × 200g serving) / 100 / 100 = 5.45, rounded to 5.4. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for these nutrients is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F02050009 and is not available in any other approved source for this specific composite dessert as of 2026: monounsaturated_fat_g — fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish; polyunsaturated_fat_g — fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB for this dish; trans_fat_g — trans fat not reported by HPB for this dish; potassium_mg — not reported in HPB F02050009; vitamin_c_mg — not reported in HPB F02050009; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — not reported in HPB F02050009; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — not reported in HPB F02050009; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — not reported in HPB F02050009; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — not reported in HPB F02050009; vitamin_b6_mg — not reported in HPB F02050009; vitamin_b12_mcg — not reported in HPB F02050009; folate_mcg_dfe — not reported in HPB F02050009; vitamin_d_mcg — not reported in HPB F02050009; vitamin_e_mg — not reported in HPB F02050009; phosphorus_mg — not reported in HPB F02050009; magnesium_mg — not reported in HPB F02050009; zinc_mg — not reported in HPB F02050009; selenium_mcg — not reported in HPB F02050009; water_g — moisture content not reported in HPB F02050009. Source consulted and confirmed unavailable: HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/), accessed 2026-04-16.
